thread.title: North Dakota national monument proposed at Badlands
thread.title_full: North Dakota national monument proposed at Badlands

author: Joey Harris
title: North Dakota national monument proposed at Badlands
text:
A coalition of North Dakota Native American advocacy and conservation groups is making a push for the federal government to designate thousands of acres of federal land along the Maah Daah Hey Trail as a national monument.
The monument would consist of 11 separate parcels, totaling around 139,000 acres along the trail connecting the north and south units of Theodore Roosevelt National Park. Dakota Prairie Grasslands -- a branch of the U.S. Forest Service -- manages the land.
The idea for a monument has been decades in the making, but this most recent effort has taken around two years and has involved putting together a history of the area, taking trips to Washington, D.C., to meet with federal agencies, and securing the written support of the Mandan, Hidatsa and Arikara Nation, the Spirit Lake Nation and the Standing Rock Sioux Tribe.
For some who are making the push, the history goes back even further.
People are also reading…
"Although Maah Daah Hey Trail is a contemporary creation, it lies within what we would conceptualize as the interior, the cultural landscape, really the bundle territory. It was something that Bears Arm in the early 20th century described as 'The earth naming bundle territory, the epicenter of the Hidatsa ceremonial life,'" said Mike Barthelemy, director of Native American Studies at Nueta Hidatsa Sahnish College in New Town. "It traverses this storied and peopled landscape and as you move through the landscape, there's all of these markers, topographic markers and in each marker there's stories."
The phrase "Maah Daah Hey" comes from the Mandan language and means "grandfather," or "an area that has been or will be around for a long time," according to the U.S. Forest Service and the Maah Daah Hey Trail Association.
There are over 150 national monuments across the country but none in North Dakota. A national monument designation gives parcels of land greater conservation protection. Hunting, biking and a number of other uses are generally allowed at national monuments. The group said the monument would also be open to cattle grazing.
A spot can be designated as a national monument either through the U.S. Congress or by a president under the Antiquities Act of 1906, a law established by former President Theodore Roosevelt, who ranched in the western North Dakota Badlands.
Industrial development in western North Dakota has significantly increased over the last two decades as the state oversaw an oil boom. Today, a number of oil rigs, pipelines, roads and bridges have the potential to further impact the landscape, according to the group.
The 11 parcels of land where the monument would go do not have private development on them, which could help clear up some of the potential complications, said John Bradley, executive director at the North Dakota Wildlife Federation.
"Following the designation of the Maah Daah Hey National Monument, the Forest Service would develop a new management plan with existing public and private partnerships as well as co-stewardship with the tribes," he said.
Scott Skokos, executive director at the Dakota Resource Council, said federal agencies have been receptive to the idea of the monument in talks.
"Obviously Joe Biden is the president until Jan. 20. We're going to be asking Joe Biden today for that specific reason, but we are not opposed to asking the next administration for the same exact designation because we believe this is a great idea no matter who is president," he said.
If the group cannot get the Biden administration to declare the area as a national monument, state Rep. Lisa Finley-DeVille, D-Mandaree, said she thinks that with outgoing North Dakota Gov. Doug Burgum joining President-elect Donald Trump's Cabinet, the group will have an ally in the White House. Trump is nominating Burgum for Interior secretary.
Part of the reason the group is pushing for the creation of a national monument is that it would give them an opportunity to tell a more complete story about the people who have lived in and around the Badlands, said Nicole Donaghy, executive director of North Dakota Native Vote.
"We all know that in western North Dakota it is very much the great conservationist Theodore Roosevelt's history (that) is laid on that land," she said. "But we must also remember that there are multiple tribes in the state that have cultural and historical ties to all over North Dakota, specifically to the Maah Daah Hey area."
Bradley, with the North Dakota Wildlife Federation, said learning this history has been eye-opening.
"I got involved in this campaign simply for the protection of the grasslands, the protection of wildlife habitat in the Badlands, that was my main focus. Over these two years, that has really changed and now my focus is taking this step back and letting our tribal leaders lead on this issue and tell their story," he said.
"North Dakota is over 90% private land. The Badlands of western North Dakota give an equal playing field, an equal opportunity to everyone in the state and the nation to challenge themselves," Bradley said. "Whether you're hiking or biking, whether you're horseback riding, whether you have a coveted once-in-a-lifetime elk (hunting) tag, the western Badlands hold a special place."
